questions.question: The term oxidation was coined for the class of chemical reactions wherein one of the following options take place?
questions.question_raw: The term oxidation was coined for the class of chemical reactions wherein one of the following options take place?

options.label: A
options.text: Addition of electropositive element to a substance.

options.label: B
options.text: Addition of oxygen to a substance

options.label: C
options.text: Removal of the electronegative element from a substance.

options.label: D
options.text: Addition of hydrogen to a substance

correct_option_texts: Addition of oxygen to a substance
questions.explanation:
Oxidation means the addition of oxygen, removal of hydrogen, increase in oxidation number, the addition of an electronegative element or removal of an electropositive element.
 ​​​​​​The term oxidation was first used to describe chemical reactions in which oxygen was added to an element of a compound. Later on, the term oxidation was extended to describe many more reactions that occurred without the use of even oxygen.
 Addition of Oxygen: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which oxygen is added to any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 Removal of Hydrogen: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which hydrogen is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 Addition of an Electronegative Element: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which an electronegative element is added into any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 Removal of an Electropositive Element: Oxidation is a chemical reaction in which an electropositive element is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 A substance that brings oxidation is known as oxidizing agents.

questions.question: Does the term reduction have one of the following options taking place?
questions.question_raw: Does the term reduction have one of the following options taking place?

options.label: A
options.text: Removal of the electropositive element from a substance

options.label: B
options.text: Removal of hydrogen to a substance

options.label: C
options.text: Addition of an electronegative element to a substance

options.label: D
options.text: Removal of oxygen from a substance

correct_option_texts: Removal of oxygen from a substance
questions.explanation:
The reduction is addition of hydrogen, removal of oxygen, removal of an electronegative element or addition of an electropositive element.
 Removal of Oxygen: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which oxygen is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 CuO + C $\longrightarrow$ Cu + CO
 H_2O + C $\longrightarrow$ CO + H_2
 2CO + O_2 $\longrightarrow$ 2CO_2
 Fe_3O_4 + 4H_2 $\longrightarrow$ 3F_2 + 4H_2O
 Addition of Hydrogen: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which hydrogen is added to any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 Cl_2 + H_2 $\longrightarrow$ 2HCl
 S + H_2 $\longrightarrow$ H_2S
 C_2H_4 + H_2 $\longrightarrow$ C_2H_6
 Removal of an Electronegative Element: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which an electronegative element is removed from any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 2HgCl_2 + SnCl_2 $\longrightarrow$ Hg_2Cl_2 + SnCl_4 (Reduction of mercuric chloride)
 2FeCl_3 + H_2 $\longrightarrow$ 2FeCl_2 + 2HCl (Reduction of ferric chloride)
 2FeCl_3 + H_2S $\longrightarrow$ 2FeCl_2 + 2HCl + S (Reduction of ferric chloride)
 Addition of an Electropositive Element: Reduction is a chemical reaction in which an electropositive element is added any chemical species (atom, ion or molecule).
 HgCl_2 + Hg $\longrightarrow$ Hg_2Cl_2 (Reduction of mercuric chloride)
 CuCl_2 + Cu $\longrightarrow$ Cu_2Cl_2 (Reduction of cupric chloride)
 The substance which brings reduction is known as a reducing agent.
 A substance, which undergoes oxidation, acts as a reducing agent while a substance, which undergoes reduction, acts as an oxidizing agent.
Mg, S, Cu, Na_2SO_3, H_2S, HI, H_2, C, KI are reducing agents, while O_2, Cl_2, F_2, H_2O_2, MnO_2, FeCl_3, CuCl_2, Fe_3O_4, CuO, etc., are oxidizing agents in the above examples.
 All oxidation and reduction reactions are complimentary of one another and occur simultaneously, one cannot take place without the other.
 No single oxidation and no single reduction process is known. The simultaneously oxidation and reduction reactions are generally termed as redox reactions. e.g., 2FeCl_3 + SnCl_2 $\longrightarrow$ 2FeCl_2 + SnCl_4.
In above example iron undergoes reduction from +3 to +2.

questions.question: Oxidation and reduction always occur simultaneously, hence, the word “redox” was coined for this class of chemical reactions. In the following redox reaction, identify the species undergoing oxidation and reduction: 3Fe_3O_4(s) + 8 Al (s) $\longrightarrow$ 9 Fe (s) + 4Al_2O_3(s)
questions.question_raw: Oxidation and reduction always occur simultaneously, hence, the word “redox” was coined for this class of chemical reactions. In the following redox reaction, identify the species undergoing oxidation and reduction: 3Fe3O4(s) + 8 Al (s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 9 Fe (s) + 4Al2O3(s)

options.label: A
options.text: Fe_3O_4 is oxidised, Fe is reduced

options.label: B
options.text: Al_2O_3 is reduced, Fe_3O_4 is oxidised

options.label: D
options.text: Fe_3O_4 is oxidised, Aluminium is reduced

questions.explanation: In the above reaction, oxygen is transferred from Fe_3O_4 to Al, so Fe_3O_4 is reduced while Al is oxidised. As per the definition of oxidation and reduction, loss of oxygen i.e reduction occurs in Fe_3O_4, and gain of oxygen i.e Oxidation occurs in Al.

questions.question:
The formation of sodium chloride involves two half-reactions, which clearly shows the involvement of electrons.
2Na (s) $\longrightarrow$ 2Na + (g) + 2e
Cl_2 (g) + 2e $\longrightarrow$ 2 Cl(g)
Sum of the half reactions gives
2 Na(s) + Cl_2(g) $\longrightarrow$ 2 Na + Cl (s) or 2 NaCl (s)
In terms of electron-transfer change, name the oxidising agent(s) and reducing agent(s).
questions.question_raw:
The formation of sodium chloride involves two half-reactions, which clearly shows the involvement of electrons.
2Na (s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Na + (g) + 2e
Cl2 (g) + 2e  {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}  2 Cl(g)
Sum of the half reactions gives
2 Na(s) + Cl2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}  2 Na + Cl (s) or 2 NaCl (s)
In terms of electron-transfer change, name the oxidising agent(s) and reducing agent(s).

options.label: A
options.text: Oxidising agents: Chlorine, oxygen and reducing agents: sodium and sulphur

options.label: B
options.text: Oxidising agent: Chlorine and Reducing agent: Sodium

options.label: C
options.text: Reducing agents: Chlorine, oxygen and oxidising agents: sodium and sulphur

options.label: D
options.text: Reducing agents: Chlorine, oxygen and sulphur and oxidising agent: sodium

correct_option_texts: Oxidising agent: Chlorine and Reducing agent: Sodium
questions.explanation:
According to the modern concept, the loss of electrons is oxidation whereas the gain of electrons is reduction. Redox reaction involves two half-reactions, one involving loss of electron or electrons (oxidation) and the other involving gain of electrons or electrons (reduction)
 Oxidizing agent is a species that can gain one or more electrons.
 Reducing agent is a species which can lose one or more electrons.
In the given reaction Sodium(Na) is losing 2 electrons, thus it is the reducing agent. Chlorine(Cl) is gaining 2 electrons, thus it is an oxidising agent.

questions.question: Copper nitrate is a blue coloured solution. Place a strip of metallic zinc in an aqueous solution of copper nitrate for about one hour. What happens?
questions.question_raw: Copper nitrate is a blue coloured solution. Place a strip of metallic zinc in an aqueous solution of copper nitrate for about one hour. What happens?

options.label: A
options.text: The zinc strip becomes coated with blue colour

options.label: B
options.text: The blue colour of the solution turns to red

options.label: C
options.text: The aqueous solution of copper nitrate turns green in colour and the metallic zinc strip turns darker in colour.

options.label: D
options.text: The blue colour of the solution becomes more intense blue.

correct_option_texts: The aqueous solution of copper nitrate turns green in colour and the metallic zinc strip turns darker in colour.
questions.explanation:
On placing a strip of metallic zinc in an aqueous solution of copper nitrate for about one hour, the copper nitrate solution turns green in colour and zinc strip turns darker.
Formula and Ionic equation for the reaction are as follows.
 Formula Equation:
 Zn (s) + Cu(NO_3)_2 (aq) $\longrightarrow$ Cu (s) + Zn(NO_3)_2 (aq)
 Ionic Equation:
 Zn (s) + Cu^2+ (aq) $\longrightarrow$ Zn^2+ (aq) + Cu (s)
As is seen in ionic equation Zn is losing 2 electrons, thus oxidation occurs and Cu is gaining 2 electrons, thus reduction occurs. Since oxidation and reduction take place simultaneously in this reaction, it is a redox reaction. Also since Zn is losing electrons and becoming darker in colour, it is the reducing agent and Cu is gaining 2 electrons, it is the reducing agent. The resultant solution Zinc nitrate [Zn(NO_3)_2] is green in colour.

questions.question: Redox reaction between copper and an aqueous solution of silver nitrate happens when the copper rod is dipped in silver nitrate solution. What happens in the final stage?
questions.question_raw: Redox reaction between copper and an aqueous solution of silver nitrate happens when the copper rod is dipped in silver nitrate solution. What happens in the final stage?

options.label: A
options.text: Silver deposits in the solution; Ag+(aq) is reduced to Ag(s).

options.label: B
options.text: The solution turns intense blue, and silver is deposited on the copper rod.

options.label: C
options.text: Silver deposits on the rod; Ag+(aq) is oxidised to Ag(s).

options.label: D
options.text: Copper deposits in the solution; Cu(aq) is reduced to ${\text{Cu}}_2^ + $ (s).

correct_option_texts: The solution turns intense blue, and silver is deposited on the copper rod.
questions.explanation:
This is due to the formation of Cu^2+ ions in the solution illustrated in the following ionic reaction:
Cu(S) + 2Ag^+(aq) $\longrightarrow$ Cu^2+(aq) + 2Ag(s)
Here, Cu is oxidized to Cu^2+ (means releasing of 2 electrons from Cu to Cu^2+ ) and Ag^+ is reduced to Ag(s) (means gaining of 2 electrons from 2Ag^+ to 2Ag ) and thus, silver converted into a solid-state and deposited on a copper rod.

questions.question: In the reaction of metallic cobalt placed in nickel sulphate solution, there is a competition for release of electrons At equilibrium, chemical tests reveal that both Ni^2+ (aq) and Co^2+ (aq) are present at moderate concentrations. The result is that:

questions.question: Oxidation number denotes the oxidation state of an element in a compound ascertained on the basis that electron in a covalent bond belongs
questions.question_raw: Oxidation number denotes the oxidation state of an element in a compound ascertained on the basis that electron in a covalent bond belongs

options.label: B
options.text: entirely to halogens in a compound

options.label: D
options.text: entirely to transition elements if present

questions.explanation:
Oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a compound according to some rules. This number enable us to describe oxidation-reduction reactions, and balancing redox chemical reactions. When a covalent bond forms between two atoms with different electronegativities the shared electrons in the bond lie closer to the more electronegative atom eg. HCl
The oxidation number of an atom is the charge that results when the electrons in a covalent bond are assigned to the more electronegative atom. It is the charge on an atom would possess if the bonding were ionic. In HCl (above) the oxidation number for the hydrogen would be +1 and that of the Cl would be -1

options.text: in the atoms of transition elements.

correct_option_texts: across the third period in the periodic table.
questions.explanation: The highest value of oxidation number exhibited by an atom of an element generally increases across the period in the periodic table.

questions.question_raw: The electrolytic decomposition of water gives H2 and O2 in the ratio of
questions.explanation: 2 : 1 by volume

questions.question: Displacement of hydrogen from cold water is done by:
questions.question_raw: Displacement of hydrogen from cold water is done by:
questions.explanation:
A common characteristic of most Alkali metal is their ability to displace H_2 (g) from water. This is represented by their large, negative electrode potentials. In this event, the Group 1 metal is oxidized to its metal ion, and water is reduced to form hydrogen gas and hydroxide ions. The general reaction of an alkali metal (M) with H_2O (l) is given in the following equation:
2M(s) + 2H_2O(l) $\longrightarrow$ 2M + (aq) + 2OH−(aq) + H_2(g)
From this reaction it is apparent that OH- is produced, creating a basic or alkaline environment. Group 1 elements are called alkali metals because of their ability to displace H_2 (g) from water and create a basic solution. Alkali metals are also known to react violently and explosively with water. This is because enough heat is given off during the exothermic reaction to ignite the H_2(g).

questions.question: Fractional Oxidation Number exists because:
questions.question_raw: Fractional Oxidation Number exists because:

options.label: A
options.text: middle carbon in such elements has always zero oxidation state.

options.label: B
options.text: the element is present in different oxidation states.

options.label: C
options.text: electrons are never shared/transferred in that element.

options.label: D
options.text: the average oxidation state of the element is divided into two.

correct_option_texts: the element is present in different oxidation states.
questions.explanation:
The oxidation states are usually in whole numbers, but in some cases, they are in fractional numbers. Consider Fe_3O_4.
 Using rule #2, the sum of the oxidation state of all atoms is 0
 Using rule #6, the O has the oxidation state of -2. 4 $\times$ (-2) = -8
 Equation: 3Fe + (-8) = 0
 3Fe = 8
 Fe = $8 \over 3$
In most cases, atoms of the same element in a given compound have the same oxidation states, but each atom can have a different state than the other. For Fe_3O_4, two Fe atoms have an oxidation state of +3 and one of +2, which makes the total oxidation state of Fe= $8 \over 3$.
Instead of averaging the oxidation states, we must keep them separated. This is called fragmenting, which occurs if there is an ionic compound, and the ions can be separated. The oxidation state of the individual atoms in the different ions can be determined.

questions.question: The strength of a reductant/oxidant in redox systems can be determined:
questions.question_raw: The strength of a reductant/oxidant in redox systems can be determined:

options.label: A
options.text: by taking into consideration the different oxidation states of the atom in the element.

options.label: B
options.text: by taking into consideration only half-reactions involving reduction.

options.label: C
options.text: by the titration method using a redox-sensitive indicator.

options.label: D
options.text: by taking into consideration only half-reactions involving oxidation.

correct_option_texts: by the titration method using a redox-sensitive indicator.
questions.explanation: Because a redox indicator (also called an oxidation-reduction indicator) is an indicator which undergoes a definite colour change at a specific electrode potential.

questions.question: One of the following has application in electrode processes and cells:
questions.question_raw: One of the following has application in electrode processes and cells:

options.text: the different oxidation states of the atom in the element.

questions.explanation: The concept of a redox reaction is used in electrode processes and cells. Because in these processes an electrode loses or gains electrons, an electrode with a higher reduction potential undergo reduction, the electrode potential is given a positive sign if oxidation occurs at the electrode.

questions.question_raw: In Daniell cell, the direction of current is:

options.label: D

questions.explanation: Current is taken at the direction in which positive charge is flowing, but electrons are negatively charged. So, the direction of current and the direction of electron flow is the opposite.

options.label: C

questions.question: In oxygen difluoride (OF_2) and dioxygen difluoride (O_2F_2), the oxygen is assigned an oxidation number of:
questions.question_raw: In oxygen difluoride (OF2) and dioxygen difluoride (O2F2), the oxygen is assigned an oxidation number of:
questions.explanation: Oxidation state of O in OF_2 is +2 and O_2F_2 is +1. Because the number assigned to oxygen will depend upon the bonding state of oxygen.

questions.question: The oxidation number of $ - \frac{1}{2}$ is assigned to an Oxygen atom in:
questions.question_raw: The oxidation number of {tex} - \frac{1}{2}{/tex} is assigned to an Oxygen atom in:

options.label: A
options.text: When oxygen is bonded to fluorine

options.label: B
options.text: When oxygen is bonded to metals

questions.explanation: In the superoxide ion, $O_2^ - $, the oxygen has an oxidation number of $ - \frac{1}{2}$. The stability of metal superoxides depends on the size and the electropositive character of the metal. The larger the metal and the more electropositive it is, the greater the stability of its superoxide.

questions.question: Assign oxidation number to P in NaH_2PO_4.
questions.question_raw: Assign oxidation number to P in NaH2PO4.
questions.explanation:
The oxidation state of P in NaH_2PO_4 is +5.
Let the oxidation number of P be x. As we already know the oxidation number of other atoms so we can calculate that of P too.
Na = +1
H= +1 (+2 for hydrogen molecule)
O = -2
Now calculating oxidation number of P:-
1 +2 + x +(-8) = 0
On solving we get x = +5.

questions.question: Assign oxidation number to S in KAl(SO_4)_2.12H_2O:
questions.question_raw: Assign oxidation number to S in KAl(SO4)2.12H2O:
questions.explanation:
Oxidation state of S in KAl(SO_4)_2.12H_2O is +6.
As, Oxidation number of S in KAl(SO_4)_2.12 H2O can be calculated as-
1 + 3 + 2X - 16 + 0 = 0 (H_2O is neutral so oxidation No. is 0)
2X = 12
X = +6

questions.question: If an excess of P_4 is treated with F_2, then_____will be produced wherein the oxidation number of P is +3. The compound is________.
questions.question_raw: If an excess of P4 is treated with F2, then_____will be produced wherein the oxidation number of P is +3. The compound is________.
questions.explanation:
Whenever a reaction between an oxidising agent and a reducing agent is carried out, a compound of lower oxidation state is formed if the reducing agent is in excess and a compound of higher oxidation state is formed if the oxidising agent is in excess. This can be illustrated as follows: P_4 and F_2 are reducing and oxidising agents respectively. If an excess of P_4 is treated with F_2, then PF_3 will be produced, wherein the oxidation number (O.N.) of P is +3.
PF_4(excess)+F_2 $\longrightarrow$ PF_3

questions.question: Which of the following is not an example of redox reaction?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is not an example of redox reaction?
questions.explanation:
The reaction is not redox because there is no oxidation or reduction of any element (i.e. no change in oxidation state/number).
Ba in BaCl_2 is at a +2 charge because it is in group 2. On the other side of the equation Ba in BaSO_4 is still +2. Cl_2 on the first side of the equation is -2 (as 2 chlorines) and Cl is -2 on the other side as there are 2 of them.
H on both sides are both +1 (but technically +2 as there are two of them).
SO_4 does not change oxidation number and always remains -2.
So, the reaction is not a redox reaction.

questions.question: Electrochemical processes for extraction of highly reactive metal and non-metal involves one of the following. Choose the most appropriate option.
questions.question_raw: Electrochemical processes for extraction of highly reactive metal and non-metal involves one of the following. Choose the most appropriate option.
questions.explanation: The electrochemical process for extraction of metal or non-metal from its ore involves the redox reaction i.e. oxidation and reduction takes place simultaneously. For example, extraction of Na and Cl_2 from NaCl.

questions.question:
2 Na(s) $\longrightarrow$ 2Na^+ + 2e^-
2H^+ + 2 e^-​​​​_​​​​ $\longrightarrow$ H_​​​​​​2
Which is oxidizing and Reducing?
questions.question_raw:
2 Na(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Na+ + 2e-
2H+   +  2 e-​​​​​​​​ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H​​​​​​2
Which is oxidizing and Reducing?

options.label: B
options.text: Sodium is oxidised and hydrogen is reduced

options.label: D
options.text: Electronegativity of sodium determines the direction of the reaction

correct_option_texts: Sodium is oxidised and hydrogen is reduced
questions.explanation:
Oxidation is a process in which one or more electrons are lost and the oxidation number is increased. Sodium has 0 oxidation state as a reactant and +1 oxidation state as a product.
Similarly, Reduction is a process in which one or more electrons are gained and the oxidation number is reduced i.e. from +1 to 0.

questions.question_raw: For ions composed of only one atom, the oxidation number is equal to the:

options.label: A
options.text: The sum of different oxidation states

questions.explanation: By definition, the oxidation number of an atom is the charge that an atom would have if the compound was composed of ions. The oxidation number of simple ions is equal to the charge on the ion. The oxidation number of sodium in the Na^+ ion is +1, for example, and the oxidation number of chlorine in the Cl^- ion is -1.

questions.question: The oxidation number of an element in a compound is evaluated on the basis of certain rules. Which of the following rules is not correct in this respect?

options.text: In all its compounds, the oxidation number of fluorine is – 1.

options.label: B
options.text: The oxidation number of hydrogen is always +1.

options.label: C
options.text: The algebraic sum of all the oxidation numbers in a compound is zero.

options.label: D
options.text: An element in the free or the uncombined state bears oxidation number zero.

correct_option_texts: The oxidation number of hydrogen is always +1.
questions.explanation: Normally hydrogen's oxidation number is +1, except for when it is part of a binary compound (2 elements) along with a metal. For example, LiH, NaH, CaH_​​​​2, all have hydrogen with oxidation number -1.

questions.question_raw:
The more positive the value of {tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex}, the greater is the tendency of the species to get reduced. Using the standard electrode potential of redox couples given below to find out which of the following is the strongest oxidising agent.
{tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} values: {tex}\frac{{{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{3 + }}}}}}{{{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{/tex} = +0.77; {tex}\frac{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{(s)}}}}{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{ - }}}}}{/tex} = +0.54;
{tex}\frac{{{\text{C}}{{\text{u}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{{{\text{Cu}}}}{/tex} = +0.34; {tex}\frac{{{\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}}}}{{{\text{Ag}}}}{/tex}= +0.80 V
questions.explanation: The more positive the value of $E^{\ominus}$, the greater is the tendency of the species to get reduced, therefore $\frac{{{\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}}}}{{{\text{Ag}}}}$^ = +0.80 is the strongest oxidizing agent.

questions.question_raw:
{tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} values of some redox couples are given below. On the basis of these values choose the correct option. 
{tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} values: {tex}\frac{{{\text{B}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{\text{B}}{{\text{r}}^{\text{ - }}}}}{/tex} = +1.90; {tex}\frac{{{\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}}}}{{{\text{Ag}}}}{/tex} = +0.80;
{tex}\frac{{{\text{C}}{{\text{u}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{{{\text{Cu}}}}{/tex} = +0.34; {tex}\frac{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{(s)}}}}{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{ - }}}}}{/tex} = +0.54
questions.explanation: Because in electrochemical series Cu is above all in sequence I_2, Ag and Br_2, therefore, Cu will reduce Br_2.

questions.question_raw:
Using the standard electrode potential, find out the pair between which redox reactions is not feasible.
{tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} values:  {tex}\frac{{{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{3 + }}}}}}{{{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{/tex} = +0.77; {tex}\frac{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{I}}^{\text{ - }}}}}{/tex}(s) = +0.54;
{tex}\frac{{{\text{C}}{{\text{u}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}}{{{\text{Cu}}}}{/tex} = +0.34; {tex}\frac{{{\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}}}}{{{\text{Ag}}}}{/tex} = +0.80V
questions.explanation:
2Ag $\rightarrow$ 2Ag^+1 + 2e^- $E^{\ominus}$ =- 0.80 V
2Fe_3+ + 2e^- $\rightarrow$ 2Fe^+2 $E^{\ominus}$ = +0.77 V
On adding the values we get,
$E^{\ominus}$cell = -0.03 V
$E^{\ominus}$ a cell is an electromotive force (also called cell voltage or cell potential) between two half-cells. The greater the
$E^{\ominus}$ a cell of a reaction the greater the driving force of electrons through the system, the more likely the reaction will proceed.
Thus, the reaction will not proceed as standard cell potential is less than zero.

questions.question: An oxidation number of +2 is found in all their compounds of one of the below-given options:
questions.question_raw: An oxidation number of +2 is found in all their compounds of one of the below-given options:
questions.explanation: Alkaline earth metals have in common an outer s- electron shell which is full; that is, that is why orbital contains its full complement of two electrons, which these elements readily lose to form cations with charge +2, and an oxidation state (oxidation number) of +2.

questions.question: The oxidizing power of halogens increase in the order of:
questions.question_raw: The oxidizing power of halogens increase in the order of:
questions.explanation:
Halogens have high electronegativity and electron affinity. They have a greater tendency to accept electrons or easily reduced, therefore they are a strong oxidizing agent.
As the reduction potential decrease down the group, the oxidizing power decrease down the group the order of the oxidizing power will be as under
F_2 > Cl_2> Br_2> I_2
The oxidizing power depends on,
The heat of dissociation of halogen molecule.
Electron affinity of the atom.
Hydration energy of the ion.
Heat of vaporization
If a halogen has low energy of dissociation, a high electron affinity, and higher hydration of its ion, it will have high oxidizing power.
F has although low electron affinity than Cl but low dissociation energy and has high hydration energy of its ion, therefore Fluorine is the strongest oxidizing agent.

NH_4^+NO_3^-.In NH_4^+

questions.question: Which of the following electrodes will act as anodes, when connected to Standard Hydrogen Electrode?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following electrodes will act as anodes, when connected to Standard Hydrogen Electrode?
questions.explanation: The anode is where oxidation occurs means species that undergo reaction at the anode should have negative electrode potential (reduction). A large negative value (e.g. Al/Al^3+ E^0 = -1.66) means that the element or compound ionizes easily, in other words, it releases electrons easily. This element or compound is easily oxidised and is, therefore, a good reducing agent.

questions.question: The decomposition of hydrogen peroxide to form water and oxygen is an example of:
questions.question_raw: The decomposition of hydrogen peroxide to form water and oxygen is an example of:
questions.explanation:
$\mathop {2{H_2}}\limits_{ + 1} \mathop {{O_2}(}\limits_{ - 1} aq) \to \mathop {2{H_2}}\limits_{ + 1} \mathop O\limits_{ - 2} + \mathop {{O_2}}\limits_0 $
Here the oxygen of peroxide, which is present in -1 state, is converted to zero oxidation state in O_2 and decreases to -2 oxidation state in H_2O.

questions.question:
Identify the correct statements with reference to the given reaction as follows:
 ${P_4} + 3O{H^ - } \to P{H_3} + 3{H_2}PO_2^ - $.
questions.question_raw:
Identify the correct statements with reference to the given reaction as follows:
 {tex}{P_4} + 3O{H^ - } \to P{H_3} + 3{H_2}PO_2^ - {/tex}.

options.label: A
options.text: Phosphorus is undergoing oxidation as well as reduction.

options.label: C
options.text: Hydrogen is undergoing oxidation as well as reduction.

correct_option_texts: Phosphorus is undergoing oxidation as well as reduction.
questions.explanation:
$\mathop {{P_4}}\limits_0 + 3O{H^ - } \to \mathop {P{H_3}}\limits_{ - 3} + \mathop {3{H_2}PO_2^ - }\limits_{ + 1} $
Phosphorus_ is undergoing oxidation as well as reduction. As the oxidation number of P_ is 0 in the reactant and in the product it is -3 as well as +1.

questions.question: The electron releasing tendency of the metals, zinc, copper, and silver is in the order:
questions.question_raw: The electron releasing tendency of the metals, zinc, copper, and silver is in the order:
questions.explanation: The electron releasing tendency of the metals, zinc, copper, and silver is in the order Zn > Cu > Ag. This is according to the metal activity series or electrochemical series.

questions.question: A complex compound in which the oxidation number of metal is zero is:
questions.question_raw: A complex compound in which the oxidation number of metal is zero is:
questions.explanation:
The oxidation number is the charge left on the central atom when all the bonding pairs of electrons are removed, with the charge devolved to the most electronegative atom.
For Ni(CO)_4, we break the Ni−CO bond and we get $Ni^0 +4×^−: C≡ O^+. $ And thus nickel has a formal oxidation state of 0.
Because Ni(CO)_4 is quite volatile, its formation forms the basis for the Mond process for nickel purification.

questions.question: Identify the correct statement (s) in relation to the following reaction: ${\text{Zn }} + {\text{ 2HCl }} \to {\text{ ZnC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}$
questions.question_raw: Identify the correct statement (s) in relation to the following reaction: {tex}{\text{Zn }} + {\text{ 2HCl }} \to {\text{ ZnC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex}

options.label: A
options.text: Chlorine is acting as a reductant

options.label: B
options.text: Hydrogen ion is acting as an oxidant

options.label: C
options.text: Zinc is acting as an oxidant

options.label: D
options.text: Hydrogen ion is acting as a reductant

correct_option_texts: Hydrogen ion is acting as an oxidant
questions.explanation:
Zn→ Zn^2+ and H------> H_2, it means that Zn was oxidized since it lost electrons, and H was reduced since it gained electrons. (Cl is a spectator ion here as its oxidation state does not change).
So then by definition, Zn is the reducing agent and H is the oxidizing agent (Oxidant).

questions.question: The standard emf of a galvanic cell involving 2 moles of electrons in its redox reaction is 0.59 V. The equilibrium constant for the redox reaction of the cell is______.
questions.question_raw: The standard emf of a galvanic cell involving 2 moles of electrons in its redox reaction is 0.59 V. The equilibrium constant for the redox reaction of the cell is______.
questions.explanation:
constant K_c can be calculated as
here, no. of moles of electrons = 2
$E^0cell = {0.059 \over n}logKc$ or $0.59 = {0.059 \over 2}logKc$
or $logKc= {0.59\times2 \over 0.059}= 20$
Kc = antilog 20 = 10^20
Kc = 10^20

questions.question: Dalda is prepared from oils by________.
questions.question_raw: Dalda is prepared from oils by________.
questions.explanation: Oils are esters of unsaturated fatty acids whereas Dalda is an ester of saturated fatty acids. The former is converted into the latter by catalytic hydrogenation i.e., reduction.

questions.question: Formation of a coloured solution is possible when metal ion in the compound contains:
questions.question_raw: Formation of a coloured solution is possible when metal ion in the compound contains:
questions.explanation: For any salt or solution to exhibit any colour, the central atom of the compound should have at least an unpaired d electron in it. This unpaired d electron is responsible for its colour in the solutions and salts.

questions.question: The standard electrode potential for a couple is +0.15 V and that for the ${\text{C}}{{\text{r}}^{{\text{3}} + }}$/Cr couple is –0.74 V. These two couples in their standard state are connected to make a cell. The cell potential will be________.
questions.question_raw: The standard electrode potential for a couple is +0.15 V and that for the {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}^{{\text{3}} + }}{/tex}/Cr couple is –0.74 V. These two couples in their standard state are connected to make a cell. The cell potential will be________.
questions.explanation:
E_cell = E_cathod - E_anode
Anodic half cell will be Cr^3+/Cr.
The cathodic half cell will be Sn^4+/Sn^2+.
So, E_cell = 0.15+0.74 = +0.89 V

questions.question: Standard electrode potential of three metals X, Y and Z are –1.2 V, +0.5 V and –3.0 V respectively. The reducing power of these metals will be:
questions.question_raw: Standard electrode potential of three metals X, Y and Z are –1.2 V, +0.5 V and –3.0 V respectively. The reducing power of these metals will be:
questions.explanation:
E^0_x= –1.2 V; E^0_y=+0.5 V; E^0_z= -3.0 V
Because higher the reduction potential, lesser the reducing power.
Therefore, Z > X > Y

questions.question:
$6{I^ - }(aq) + 2MnO_4^ - (aq) + 4{H_2}O(I) $ $\longrightarrow$ $3{I_2}(s) + 2MnO(s) + 8O{H^ - }(aq)$
The above balanced ionic equation represents a redox reaction where one of the following takes place.
questions.question_raw:
{tex}6{I^ - }(aq) + 2MnO_4^ - (aq) + 4{H_2}O(I) {/tex}  {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}3{I_2}(s) + 2MnO(s) + 8O{H^ - }(aq){/tex}
The above balanced ionic equation represents a redox reaction where one of the following takes place.

options.label: A
options.text: iodide ion in basic solution oxidizes permanganate ion

options.label: B
options.text: $MnO_4^ - $ in basic solution oxidizes I^- to produce I_2

options.label: C
options.text: permanganate ion in basic solution oxidizes water

options.label: D
options.text: permanganate (VII) ion in basic solution reduces iodide ion to produce molecular iodine

correct_option_texts: $MnO_4^ - $ in basic solution oxidizes I^- to produce I_2
questions.explanation: Mn in $MnO_4^ - $ (Oxidation state = +7) is itself getting reduced to MnO_2 (+4) and I^​​​​​​- (Oxidation state = -1) is getting oxidized to I_2 (Oxidation state = 0).

questions.question: Metal in a compound can be displaced by another metal in the uncombined state. Which metal is a better reducing agent in such a case?
questions.question_raw: Metal in a compound can be displaced by another metal in the uncombined state. Which metal is a better reducing agent in such a case?

options.label: A
options.text: The reduced metal is a better reducing agent than the reducing metal

options.label: B
options.text: Both are the same in reducing capacity

options.label: C
options.text: A better reducing agent is the one that loses more electrons

options.label: D
options.text: A better reducing agent is the one that loses fewer electrons

correct_option_texts: A better reducing agent is the one that loses more electrons
questions.explanation: The metal causing displacement is a better reducing agent than the one that is being replaced. In simple words, such a metal shows more capability to loose electrons as compared to the one that is reduced.

questions.question: Hydrogen is prepared from H_2O by adding:
questions.question_raw: Hydrogen is prepared from H2O by adding:

options.label: A
options.text: Ca, which acts as a reducing agent.

options.label: B
options.text: AI, which acts as oxidising agent.

options.label: C
options.text: Ag, which acts as a reducing agent.

options.label: D
options.text: Au, which acts as oxidising agent.

correct_option_texts: Ca, which acts as a reducing agent.
questions.explanation:
Calcium is a silvery-white metal; it is relatively soft but much harder than sodium metal. Calcium is a member of the alkaline-earth metals (Group II on the periodic table); these metals react vigorously with water, although not as violently as the Group I metals such as sodium or potassium:
Ca(s) + 2H_2O(l) $\longrightarrow$ Ca(OH)_2(aq) + H_2(g)

questions.question: In the conversion of Br_2 to Bro^3-, the oxidation number of Br changes from:
questions.question_raw: In the conversion of Br2 to Bro3-, the oxidation number of Br changes from:
questions.explanation: In Br_2 oxidation number of Br is 0 while in BrO_3 ^-Br has an oxidation number of +5 as each O carries a -2 charge and the net charge is -1.

questions.question: In the reaction 2H_2O_2 $\rightarrow$ 2H_2O + O_2?
questions.question_raw: In the reaction 2H2O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H2O + O2?

options.label: B
options.text: Oxygen is neither oxidised nor reduced

options.label: C
options.text: Oxygen is oxidised as well as reduced

correct_option_texts: Oxygen is oxidised as well as reduced
questions.explanation: This is a disproportionate reaction. (Disproportionation is a specific type of redox reaction in which an element from a reaction undergoes both oxidation and reduction to form two different products).

questions.question:
Consider the elements: Cs, Ne, I and F.
identify the element(s) that exhibits only a negative oxidation state?
questions.question_raw:
Consider the elements: Cs, Ne, I and F.
identify the element(s) that exhibits only a negative oxidation state?
questions.explanation: Fluorine is more electronegative as it belongs to group 17 or 7 and can show only a negative oxidation state of -1.

questions.question: In which of the following compounds, an element exhibits two different oxidation states?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following compounds, an element exhibits two different oxidation states?
questions.explanation:
NH_4NO_3 is an ionic compound consisting of NH_4^+ and NO_3^- ion. The oxidation number of N in two species is different as shown below:
In the case of NH_4^+,
Let the oxidation state of N in NH_4^+ be x.
x + 4 $\times$ (+1) = +1
x = -3
In the NO_3^-_,
Let the oxidation state of N in NO_3^- be y,
y + 3 $\times$ (-2) = -1
y = +5
Hence, NH_4NO_3 exhibits two different oxidation states.

questions.question: Consider the elements: Cs, Ne, I and F. Identify the element that exhibits both positive and negative oxidation states.
questions.question_raw: Consider the elements: Cs, Ne, I and F. Identify the element that exhibits both positive and negative oxidation states.
questions.explanation: Iodine has 7 electrons in its valency shell and can, therefore, exhibit an oxidation state of –1, However since Iodine has empty d orbitals it can also exhibit different oxidation state like +1,+3,+5, +7.

questions.question: Consider the elements: Cs, Ne, I and F. Identify the element(s) which exhibits neither the negative nor does the positive oxidation state.
questions.question_raw: Consider the elements: Cs, Ne, I and F. Identify the element(s) which exhibits neither the negative nor does the positive oxidation state.
questions.explanation: Since, Ne is noble gas having stable electronic configuration i.e. 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6. So it do not show any positive or negative oxidation state.

questions.question: In Ostwald’s process for the manufacture of nitric acid, the first step involves the oxidation of ammonia gas by oxygen gas to give nitric oxide gas and steam. What is the maximum weight of nitric oxide that can be obtained starting only with 10.00 g of ammonia and 20.00 g of oxygen?
questions.question_raw: In Ostwald’s process for the manufacture of nitric acid, the first step involves the oxidation of ammonia gas by oxygen gas to give nitric oxide gas and steam. What is the maximum weight of nitric oxide that can be obtained starting only with 10.00 g of ammonia and 20.00 g of oxygen?
questions.explanation:
The reaction that takes place in ammonia and oxygen is given below:
$\mathop {4N{H_3}}\limits_{68g} + \mathop {5{O_2}}\limits_{160g} \to \mathop {4NO}\limits_{120g} + 6{H_2}O$
Limiting reagent
160g of oxygen reacts with 68g of ammonia
20g of oxygen reacts with $\frac{68\times20}{160}
$ = 8.5g of ammonia
Therefore for 20g of oxygen 8.5g of ammonia is used. Therefore 1.5g ammonia is in excess and therefore oxygen is the limiting reagent.
160 g of O_2 produces = 120g of NO 
Therefore, 20 g of O_2 produces= $\frac{120\times20}{60}$ = 15 g of NO.
